name: AltID platform-neutral trust anchor standards
consequences: Immediate: The dependency surface for MitID and related services becomes observable and auditable, enabling targeted interventions. Systemic: Platform-neutral fallback readiness grows to 40 percentage points, with reliance on Apple/Google signals dropping from 95% to 55%, reducing lock-in by 40 percentage points. Strategic: Strengthens Denmark’s sovereignty and procurement leverage, enabling policy-driven diversification, but increases governance overhead and short-term integration costs as agencies harmonize standards and signals across ecosystems.
options: Publish a standards-backed dependency surface map and require Digitaliseringsstyrelsen to adopt platform-neutral procurement criteria for AltID-related components.
Pilot a cross-sector, platform-neutral authentication architecture using FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect with GrapheneOS, establishing a formal fallback path within public-sector test environments.

Launch a community-owned, open-source platform-neutral trust anchor leveraging self-sovereign identity (DID) with portable wallets and hardware-backed attestations, financed via a new procurement-as-a-service model.
review: Controls platform-dependence vs. procurement-neutral sovereignty. Weakness: The options fail to consider the political economy and resistance from incumbent operators within procurement ecosystems.

name: Procurement language for platform-neutral fallback authentication
consequences: Immediate: Policy language formalizes platform-neutrality as a required criterion in planning and tender design. Systemic: In 24 months, 60% of AltID-related tenders adopt platform-neutral criteria; platform lock-in exposure declines by ~30 percentage points (Apple/Google signal reliance from 90% to 60%). Strategic: Expands policy influence and reduces vendor lock-in risk, but may slow procurement cycles and provoke resistance from incumbents who benefit from current arrangements.
options: Draft and socialize procurement amendments requiring vendor-agnostic fallback authentication in all critical public digital services.
Lobby for formal Digitaliseringsstyrelsen-led policy language mandating platform-neutrality criteria in AltID procurement and public tenders.
Create a public-private consortium to certify and manage a platform-neutral fallback authentication service with open-source compliance, financed by subscription from public agencies.
review: Controls platform-dependence vs. procurement-neutral sovereignty. Weakness: The options fail to consider the political economy and resistance from incumbent operators within procurement ecosystems.

name: Coalition governance and continuity demonstration
consequences: Immediate: A governance charter is enacted, specifying coalition membership, decision gates, and milestone commitments. Systemic: Stakeholder engagement expands to 20+ organisations and formal interoperability protocols are drafted; two non-production demonstrators progress toward credible live-comparable results. Strategic: Strengthens resilience and EU-interoperability posture while increasing process complexity and potential decision bottlenecks if gates are too rigid or timelines slip.
options: Establish a governance charter detailing coalition membership, decision gates, and milestones; publish quarterly risk-and-progress reports.
Run two non-production demonstrators under a shared, open governance framework to compare platform-neutral fallback performance against current MitID signals.
Set up an independent, multi-stakeholder continuity laboratory that certifies interoperability across EU wallets and negotiates cross-border fallback standards.
review: Controls platform-dependence vs. procurement-neutral sovereignty. Weakness: The options fail to consider the political economy and resistance from incumbent operators within procurement ecosystems.

name: EU interoperability alignment and wallet standards pressure
consequences: Immediate: EU-aligned interoperability positions are prepared, increasing visibility in Brussels and national policy debate. Systemic: Danish inputs contribute to EU wallet standards; alignment improves by an estimated 25 percentage points, reducing the need for later rework and easing cross-border onboarding. Strategic: Elevates Denmark’s influence in EU wallet development and reduces risk of policy friction, but requires negotiation among diverse national interests and acceptance of shared certification regimes.
options: Engage with EU working groups to align with existing Digital Identity Wallet requirements and report back on potential interoperability gaps.
Submit formal EU policy inputs encouraging a platform-neutral fallback path and push for NSIS-aligned, open standards in national procurement.
Establish a Danish-EU interoperability council that pilots cross-border fallback access with common trust anchors and shared certification regimes.
review: Controls platform-dependence vs. procurement-neutral sovereignty. Weakness: The options fail to consider the variance in EU member-state readiness and the political sensitivity of mutual recognition across borders.

name: Resilience engineering and fallback readiness testing
consequences: Immediate: Resilience plans are refined through test scenarios; fallback options gain exposure in controlled exercises. Systemic: Fallback readiness increases by 25 percentage points; simulated outages demonstrate a 20% reduction in service interruptions and faster mean time to recovery. Strategic: Improves service continuity and risk posture, but requires sustained investment in resilience architecture and potential external security reviews; the focus on preparedness may compete with other modernization priorities.
options: Develop a resilience dashboard tracking dependency on platform signals, incident response times, and fallback readiness across agencies.
Institute continuous testing of fallback authentication under simulated platform outages with measurable target reductions in service interruption.
Implement a continuous, automatic failover network of alternate trust anchors, including beacon-based push-channel redundancy and edge-case offline verification.
review: Controls platform-dependence vs. procurement-neutral sovereignty. Weakness: The options fail to consider the ongoing need for real-world stress testing across diverse geographic and organizational boundaries.

name: Demonstrator portfolio governance and security assurance
consequences: Immediate: Security posture reviews are initiated and a mitigation backlog is documented. Systemic: Two non-production demonstrators deliver verifiable results; third-party security reviews are completed, reducing critical findings by roughly 30–40%. Strategic: Builds trust and accreditation credibility for platform-neutral approaches, but increases upfront cost and extends project timelines due to additional security cycles and vendor audits.
options: Document security gaps identified in phase-one demonstrators and publish a remediation backlog with owner assignments.
Advance two non-production demonstrators with integrated risk assessment and third-party security review to maintain accreditation credibility.
Prototype a hardware-backed, portable authentication token ecosystem with multi-source attestation using a distributed ledger for auditability.
review: Controls platform-dependence vs. procurement-neutral sovereignty. Weakness: The options fail to consider the risk of vendor lock-in in the security-review supply chain and the need for independent assurance providers.

name: Fallback authentication backbone for AltID integration
consequences: Immediate: Initiate a platform-agnostic fallback path to validate core authentication flows independent of Apple/Google signals. Systemic: Interoperability readiness improves by 30% across 6 of 12 critical portals, while short-term certification and integration costs rise by 18% — a trade-off between resilience gains and governance coordination overhead. Strategic: Establishes a durable platform-neutral path, enabling multi-vendor resilience and stronger regulatory credibility, though requiring sustained governance and cross-agency collaboration.
options: Establish a platform-agnostic fallback path by adopting core standards (FIDO2/WebAuthn, OpenID Connect) across critical public portals in a limited pilot.
Scale the fallback pathway to cover 3–5 essential services and initiate formal NSIS- and eIDAS-aligned procurement processes.
Deploy a decentralized identity layer using DIDs and verifiable credentials with a consortium-driven governance and a new service model that monetizes certification services.
review: Controls platform-dependency vs resilience/autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider political economy risks from incumbent operators and regulatory friction.

name: Platform-neutral procurement terms for identity services
consequences: Immediate: Procurement teams begin evaluating platform-neutral options and include fallback criteria in new RFPs. Systemic: Vendor-lock-in risk reduces by 20% and interoperability coverage increases by 25%, while negotiation cycles extend by 12% due to stricter platform-neutral requirements. Strategic: Gains in autonomy and resilience buttress long-term sovereignty, but require allocation of additional procurement resources and longer lead times.
options: Redraft procurement templates to require platform-neutral fallback authentication as a baseline for all identity-service contracts, starting with pilot entities.
Incorporate performance-based milestones linked to fallback accessibility metrics into all major identity-services procurements.
Adopt a performance-incentive, open-source certification model with subscription-based governance to ensure ongoing platform neutrality and compliance.
review: Controls platform-dependency vs resilience/autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider national-level political economy and EU timeline alignment.

name: EU wallet interoperability advocacy and standards pressure
consequences: Immediate: Danish stakeholders engage EU bodies to embed platform-neutral requirements in European Digital Identity Wallet specs, piloting cross-border verification. Systemic: Cross-border wallet interoperability improves by 40%, while Danish suppliers face longer compliance cycles and increased standardization overhead (about 15%). Strategic: Elevates Denmark as a leader in European interoperability, reducing long-term platform lock-in risk and accelerating adoption of platform-neutral trust anchors.
options: Engage with EU-level working groups to embed platform-neutrality requirements in European Digital Identity Wallet specs, piloting cross-border verification.
Push for NSIS-compatible fallback signals in EU wallet mappings and demand open-source reference implementations.
Sponsor a radical EU-wide consortial platform-neutral wallet with a tokenized incentive model and open governance.
review: Controls cross-border interoperability vs domestic procurement velocity. Weakness: The options fail to consider national-level political economy and EU timeline alignment.

name: Resilience continuity playbook for public-service access
consequences: Immediate: Initiate resilience playbook development and containment drills for platform-policy shifts. Systemic: Time-to-restore readiness improves by 25% in simulated outages; contingency budget increases by 10%. Strategic: Builds institutional muscle for rapid pivots during platform-risk events, strengthening public trust while potentially slowing rapid feature deployment due to preparedness requirements.
options: Develop a resilience playbook focusing on business continuity planning for digital identity during policy shifts and supply chain disruptions.
Create a phased continuity migration plan that includes targeted fallback deployments during platform policy changes, with clearly defined decision gates.
Institutionalize a testbed for platform-neutral fallback access with simulated outages and emergency governance triggers, supported by a new budget line.
review: Controls continuity planning vs innovation speed. Weakness: The options fail to consider budgetary volatility and long-term maintenance costs.

name: Open standards demonstrator suite and governance model
consequences: Immediate: Prototype demonstrations reveal cross-platform gaps and governance requirements. Systemic: Cross-portfolio adoption grows by 28%, while funding and management overhead increase by 15% due to open governance. Strategic: Elevates credibility with industry and policymakers, enabling wider adoption of platform-neutral approaches and reducing vendor-lock-in risk over time.
options: Launch a non-production demonstrator suite showcasing WebAuthn/OpenID Connect across multiple device platforms to demonstrate platform neutrality.
Establish an open governance model with a neutral technical board and transparent funding to steer demonstration work.
Adopt a radical open-source governance model with a tokenized funding mechanism and a subscription-based certification ecosystem to accelerate platform neutrality.
review: Controls demonstration credibility vs governance overhead. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term sustainability of open governance and risk of fragmentation in standards.

name: Independent trust-materials program for device-agnostic attestation
consequences: Immediate: Initiate device-agnostic attestation across core access points. Systemic: Attestation coverage increases by 35% across public portals, while hardware integration costs rise by 15%. Strategic: Reduces reliance on single OS ecosystems, strengthening sovereignty and reducing platform risk in the long run.
options: Institute device-agnostic attestation using hardware-backed keys and minimal OS-inspection to reduce gatekeeping.
Develop certified attestation modules for GrapheneOS, Linux, and Windows with standardized attestation certificates.
Pilot a blockchain-based attestation registry with a consortium-funded, revenue-neutral model to support cross-platform trust material.
review: Controls device-agnostic attestation coverage vs cost and supply-chain risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider hardware supply constraints and legacy equipment compatibility.

name: Platform-neutral fallback authentication architecture
consequences: Immediate: Cross-domain experiments validate platform-neutral fallback signals in controlled environments. → Systemic: 25% increase in supplier diversification index, while procurement cycles lengthen by 15%. → Strategic: The project gains resilience and strategic autonomy but incurs higher governance overhead and slower time-to-value.
options: Pilot a platform-neutral fallback authentication architecture within current MitID-adjacent workflows, using OpenID Connect and FIDO2 in sandboxed environments.
Scale the platform-neutral fallback architecture by integrating a browser-based and hardware-token option with an independent verifier.
Launch a decentralized SSI-based fallback network with verifiable credentials and a tokenized broker marketplace to certify alternative trust anchors.
review: Controls speed-to-delivery vs. platform-dependency risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider how non-European identity ecosystems and regional regulatory constraints could affect interoperability and certification timelines.

name: Procurement language harmonization for identity services
consequences: Immediate: Aligns procurement contracts with platform-neutral criteria and initiates policy review and vendor outreach. → Systemic: 20% reduction in exclusive-ecosystem dependencies across major procurements, but compliance overhead increases by 10%. → Strategic: Purchases become more resilient and portable, but short-term onboarding costs and governance complexity rise.
options: Revise procurement templates to require platform-neutral fallback capabilities with limited scope and clear evaluation criteria.
Institute a mandatory pilot inclusion of platform-neutral fallback options across multiple service lines and tighten oversight.
Create an outcome-based procurement model paired with a platform-neutral fallback certification marketplace and dynamic contracting for identity services.
review: Controls speed of procurement cycles vs. platform-dependency risk. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential for vendor capacity gaps to meet new criteria within tight cycles.

name: Coalition-building for supplier-diversity and resilience
consequences: Immediate: Establishes a cross-sector coalition that starts policy dialogue and pilot scoping. → Systemic: 30% increase in potential supplier pool, yet decision-making slows due to consensus processes. → Strategic: Greater resilience and autonomy, but slower momentum and potential dilution of accountability.
options: Form a broad coalition of civil-society, academia, and industry to advocate for supplier-diversity in identity services.
Develop formal memoranda of understanding and joint position papers to push for platform-neutral fallback in AltID procurement.
Launch a multi-stakeholder governance consortium that funds and repurposes vendor ecosystems via a public-private platform accelerator.
review: Controls stakeholder engagement breadth vs decision speed. Weakness: The options fail to address potential conflicts of interest and accountability in a multi-stakeholder coalition.

name: EU interoperability and wallet standards advocacy
consequences: Immediate: Elevates Denmark's standing in EU forums and secures initial commitments. → Systemic: 15% improvement in cross-border interoperability readiness, but EU negotiation timelines may add 6–12 months. → Strategic: Aligns with EU requirements and enables platform-neutral fallback path, but delays to-value realization.
options: Engage EU bodies with formal comments and pilot-case studies to influence wallet interoperability requirements.
Attend and contribute to European Tech Working Groups to embed platform-neutral fallback criteria in wallet framework specs.
Establish a cross-border interoperability compact tying public procurement incentives to EU wallet standards, including a platform-neutral fallback path as a certifiable requirement.
review: Controls regulatory influence vs operational autonomy. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential friction from national procurement laws and veto points within EU bodies.

name: Independent verification and security review framework for continuity
consequences: Immediate: Initiates third-party scrutiny of fallback pathways. → Systemic: 18% improvement in assurance posture, but annual audit costs rise by 20%. → Strategic: Builds trust and accountability but increases budget pressure and potential delays in procurement cycles.
options: Establish a formal security review requirement for any fallback paths with independent auditors.
Contract a rotating panel of independent assessors to validate platform-neutral candidate pathways and supply-chain security.
Launch an open-security-influence model with crowdsourced attestation and a shared transparency ledger for fallback proofs.
review: Controls assurance rigor vs speed to deploy. Weakness: The options fail to consider potential conflicts between audit cadence and procurement deadlines.

name: Open-source demonstrator program and governance
consequences: Immediate: Publishes demonstrators and invites external review. → Systemic: 22% increase in external contributors and code quality improvements, but governance coordination costs rise. → Strategic: Accelerates innovation and transparency, but risks misalignment with policy constraints and potential security concerns.
options: Publish open-source demonstrators and invite external review under permissive licenses.
Institutionalize community governance with bug-bounty programs and rapid-cycle sprints.
Create an open, globally distributed builder network with crowdfunding, bounties, and a DAO-style governance for platform-neutral fallback identity ecosystem.
review: Controls transparency vs control. Weakness: The options fail to address funding sustainability and policy alignment for open-source contributions.
